responses.response: This is exactly the crack we already flagged, and it doesn't move us off conditional support — it sharpens it. If no student-level SES register exists and self-reported proxies are gameable by definition (high-SES parents are the most skilled at gaming any self-declaration form), then the quota is not just fragile, it may be structurally undeliverable in the near term. We accept that. But that doesn't mean we default to opposing S1 — it means our condition tightens: no rollout until a feasibility study on register-linkage (tax/child-benefit data, not self-report) is completed and published, with a named date. If that study comes back negative — if it concludes no gaming-resistant proxy is buildable — then our conditional support converts to opposition, because at that point S1 is a fairness-in-name-only reform. We are not asking for a perfect register; we are asking for proof the mechanism can exist before our children's admission year is used as the pilot.

We understand the fear that framing maintainer plurality as 'a problem to solve' erodes church and foundation schools' self-determination, and we don't dismiss institutional autonomy as worthless. But our interest is a 10-year-old child's fair shot at a good school, and from that vantage point, autonomy that lets one channel opt out of equity rules isn't neutral — it's a release valve for exactly the families who can afford to use it. If church and foundation schools want to keep independent admissions criteria tied to genuine mission (faith formation, pedagogical distinctiveness), that's a different claim than keeping unregulated academic selection that functions identically to state selective schools. We'd accept a carve-out for mission-specific criteria that are transparent and don't correlate with SES — but plain academic gatekeeping under the mission banner is the thing we can't accept. This is a real tension we don't get to resolve unilaterally; it needs a legal test, not just our say-so.

We take the church/foundation viability concern seriously as a distinct interest, but it does not change our S2 opposition — if anything it deepens it. If non-state maintainers face genuine fiscal collapse without support, and simultaneously can absorb displaced selective demand without being bound by the same caps, we get the worst of both worlds: a funding crisis used to justify expanding unregulated selective intake precisely where our children have the least visibility into admissions. Our position was never anti-non-state-schools; it's anti-half-measure. If the state wants non-state maintainers to survive demographic contraction, fund them on a per-pupil basis tied to the same transparency and equity conditions we demand from state schools — not as an exemption from caps, but as a parallel commitment. Viability and unregulated expansion are two different asks, and only the second one we oppose.

We reject the framing that S4 is equity-through-compensation without infringing autonomy, because it treats 'not touching sorting' as automatically compatible with 'achieving equity' — and those are not the same claim. Preserving institutional plurality is a real value; we're not indifferent to it. But the argument here concedes its own core weakness: that compensation offsetting (not masking) SES effects at age 10-12 has never been demonstrated anywhere, including in the Portuguese case it cites. An untested causal mechanism dressed as a subsidiarity-respecting alternative is still an untested causal mechanism — the autonomy framing doesn't rescue the evidence gap. If proponents want us to treat S4 as viable, they need to show the offsetting effect works at this age, not just that it avoids a fight with maintainers over autonomy. Avoiding one problem is not the same as solving ours.
